tasks.title: Update hono to >=4.9.6 across all workspace packages
tasks.description:
Update the `hono` dependency to version >=4.9.6 in all workspace packages that declare it. This addresses CVE URL path parsing confusion (4.8.0-4.9.5) that allows middleware auth bypass.

Files to modify:
- `apps/api/package.json` (primary consumer)
- Any other `package.json` files in the monorepo that declare `hono` as a dependency

Steps:
1. Run `pnpm why hono` to identify all packages depending on hono
2. Update each `package.json` to specify hono >=4.9.6
3. Run `pnpm install` and resolve any peer dependency conflicts
4. Run `pnpm typecheck` to verify no type errors from the upgrade
5. Run `pnpm test` in `apps/api` to verify all route and middleware tests pass

Test expectations:
- All existing API tests pass without modification
- `pnpm why hono` shows only >=4.9.6 versions resolved

tasks.title: Update @tanstack/form-core to non-vulnerable version
tasks.description:
Update `@tanstack/form-core` to a version that resolves known prototype pollution advisories.

Files to modify:
- `apps/admin/package.json` (primary consumer of TanStack Form)
- Any other `package.json` files declaring `@tanstack/form-core` or `@tanstack/react-form`

Steps:
1. Run `pnpm why @tanstack/form-core` to identify all consumers
2. Check npm advisory page for the minimum safe version
3. Update the dependency version in all relevant package.json files
4. Run `pnpm install`
5. Run `pnpm typecheck` in `apps/admin`
6. Run `pnpm test` in `apps/admin` to verify no regressions

Test expectations:
- All admin app tests pass
- `pnpm audit` no longer reports this advisory

tasks.title: Update devalue to non-vulnerable version
tasks.description:
Update the `devalue` package to a version without known prototype pollution or code injection advisories.

Files to modify:
- Identify all `package.json` files declaring `devalue` via `pnpm why devalue`
- May be a transitive dependency of Astro or TanStack Start

Steps:
1. Run `pnpm why devalue` to identify direct and transitive consumers
2. If direct dependency: update version in the relevant `package.json`
3. If transitive: check if updating the parent package resolves it, or add a `pnpm.overrides` entry in root `package.json`
4. Run `pnpm install`
5. Run `pnpm test` across affected apps

Test expectations:
- All tests pass
- `pnpm audit` no longer reports devalue advisory

tasks.title: Update path-to-regexp to non-vulnerable version
tasks.description:
Update `path-to-regexp` to a version without known ReDoS (Regular Expression Denial of Service) advisories.

Files to modify:
- Identify all consumers via `pnpm why path-to-regexp`
- Likely a transitive dependency of routing libraries

Steps:
1. Run `pnpm why path-to-regexp` to identify the dependency chain
2. If direct: update version. If transitive: update parent or add `pnpm.overrides` in root `package.json`
3. Run `pnpm install`
4. Run `pnpm test` across all apps to verify routing still works

Test expectations:
- All route-related tests pass
- `pnpm audit` no longer reports path-to-regexp advisory

tasks.title: Run pnpm audit and fix remaining high/critical advisories
tasks.description:
After updating the four known vulnerable packages (T-001 through T-004), run a comprehensive audit to catch any remaining critical or high severity advisories.

Steps:
1. Run `pnpm audit --production` and capture output
2. For each remaining critical/high advisory, determine fix (version update, override, or accepted risk)
3. Apply fixes
4. Run `pnpm install`
5. Run `pnpm typecheck && pnpm lint && pnpm test` to verify no regressions
6. Run `pnpm audit --production` again and confirm zero critical/high advisories

Test expectations:
- `pnpm audit --production` reports zero critical and zero high advisories
- Full test suite passes
- Full typecheck passes

tasks.title: Rotate database credentials and update deployment secrets
tasks.description:
Rotate the PostgreSQL database credentials that were exposed in the committed `.env` file (commits `33bd4124` and `bacbf585`).

Steps:
1. Generate new database password using a cryptographically secure method
2. Update the database password on the PostgreSQL server (or managed database provider)
3. Update `HOSPEDA_DATABASE_URL` in Fly.io secrets: `fly secrets set HOSPEDA_DATABASE_URL=...`
4. Update `HOSPEDA_DATABASE_URL` in Vercel environment variables for admin and web apps
5. Verify the API can connect to the database with the new credentials
6. Verify the admin and web apps can function with the updated env vars

IMPORTANT: This is a manual/operational task. The old credential values must be considered compromised. Coordinate timing with deployment to minimize downtime.

Test expectations:
- API starts successfully with new credentials
- Database operations work normally

**DEFERRED to SPEC-024** (Credential Rotation & Git History Purge). All credential rotation tasks are tracked there.

tasks.title: Rotate Better Auth secret and update deployment secrets
tasks.description:
Rotate the Better Auth secret key that was exposed in the committed `.env` file.

Steps:
1. Generate a new `HOSPEDA_BETTER_AUTH_SECRET` using `openssl rand -base64 32` or equivalent
2. Update Fly.io secrets: `fly secrets set HOSPEDA_BETTER_AUTH_SECRET=...`
3. Update Vercel environment variables for admin and web apps
4. NOTE: Rotating this secret will invalidate all existing sessions. Users will need to re-login.
5. Verify authentication flow works with the new secret

IMPORTANT: Coordinate with the team. All active sessions will be invalidated.

Test expectations:
- Login flow works with new secret
- Session creation and validation work correctly

**DEFERRED to SPEC-024** (Credential Rotation & Git History Purge). All credential rotation tasks are tracked there.

tasks.title: Rotate all other API keys and service credentials
tasks.description:
Rotate any remaining API keys or service credentials that appeared in the committed `.env` file, beyond the database URL and Better Auth secret.

Steps:
1. Review the contents of the old `.env` (from git history if still accessible, or from records) to identify all credentials
2. For each credential (Sentry DSN, MercadoPago keys, any external service keys):
   a. Generate new key/secret with the respective provider
   b. Revoke the old key with the provider
   c. Update Fly.io secrets and Vercel env vars
3. Verify all integrations work with the new credentials

Test expectations:
- All external integrations (Sentry, billing, etc.) function correctly
- No references to old credential values remain in deployment configuration

**DEFERRED to SPEC-024** (Credential Rotation & Git History Purge). All credential rotation tasks are tracked there.

tasks.title: Purge .env from git history using git-filter-repo
tasks.description:
Remove all traces of the `.env` file from git history to prevent credential recovery from historical commits.

Steps:
1. Ensure all credentials have been rotated first (T-007, T-008, T-009)
2. Notify all contributors they will need to re-clone after this operation
3. Install `git-filter-repo` if not already available
4. Run: `git filter-repo --path .env --invert-paths`
5. Force push to the remote repository
6. All contributors must delete their local clone and re-clone

IMPORTANT: This is a destructive operation that rewrites git history. ALL contributors must re-clone afterwards. Coordinate timing carefully.

Test expectations:
- `git log --all -- .env` returns no commits
- Fresh clone shows no `.env` content in any historical commit

**DEFERRED to SPEC-024** (Credential Rotation & Git History Purge). Git history purge is tracked there.

tasks.title: Verify git history purge is clean
tasks.description:
After the git-filter-repo purge (T-010), verify that no credential values are recoverable from the repository history.

Steps:
1. Perform a fresh `git clone` of the repository to a temporary directory
2. Run `git log --all -- .env` and verify no results with credential content
3. For each known old credential value, run `git log --all -S '<old-credential-value>'` and verify no matches
4. Run `git log --all --diff-filter=A -- .env` and verify no results
5. Verify `.gitignore` contains `.env` and `.env.local` patterns
6. Document the verification results

Test expectations:
- Zero credential values recoverable from git history
- `.gitignore` properly ignores `.env` files

**DEFERRED to SPEC-024** (Credential Rotation & Git History Purge). Purge verification is tracked there.

tasks.title: Remove hardcoded password from superAdminLoader.ts and add random generation
tasks.description:
Replace the hardcoded `SuperAdmin123!` password in the seed utility with environment variable support and random password generation fallback.

File to modify:
- `packages/seed/src/utils/superAdminLoader.ts`

Steps:
1. Read `packages/seed/src/utils/superAdminLoader.ts` and locate the hardcoded password string `SuperAdmin123!`
2. Replace the hardcoded password with:
   a. Read `process.env.HOSPEDA_SUPER_ADMIN_PASSWORD`
   b. If set, use that value
   c. If NOT set, generate a cryptographically random password using `crypto.randomBytes(24).toString('base64url')` (or similar)
   d. If generated, print it to stdout once: `console.log('[SEED] Generated super admin password: <password>')`
   e. Log a warning: `console.warn('[SEED] Save this password now. It will not be shown again.')`
3. Ensure no other seed files contain hardcoded passwords (search for `SuperAdmin123` across the codebase)
4. Update `.env.example` (if already created by T-006) to include `HOSPEDA_SUPER_ADMIN_PASSWORD`

Test expectations:
- No hardcoded password strings in any seed file
- When env var is set, that value is used
- When env var is not set, a random password is generated and printed
- `grep -r 'SuperAdmin123' packages/seed/` returns no matches

tasks.title: Implement forced password change on first super admin login
tasks.description:
Add a mechanism to force the super admin to change their password on first login before accessing admin functionality.

Files to modify:
- `packages/seed/src/utils/superAdminLoader.ts` -- add a `forcePasswordChange: true` flag to the created user record (or equivalent metadata)
- `packages/db/src/schemas/user/` -- may need to add a `forcePasswordChange` column or use existing metadata fields
- `apps/admin/src/routes/_authed.tsx` -- check for forcePasswordChange flag and redirect to password change page
- `apps/admin/src/routes/change-password.tsx` -- create or update password change page
- `apps/api/` -- endpoint to handle password change and clear the flag

Steps:
1. Determine how to store the forcePasswordChange flag (new column vs. user metadata JSON)
2. Set the flag to true when the super admin account is created in the seed
3. In the admin route guard (_authed.tsx), after auth check, if forcePasswordChange is true, redirect to /change-password
4. Create the /change-password page with a form (current password, new password, confirm password)
5. On successful password change, clear the forcePasswordChange flag
6. The change-password page should be accessible even when forcePasswordChange is true
7. Add a flag to skip this in test/CI environments (e.g., `HOSPEDA_SKIP_FORCE_PASSWORD_CHANGE=true`)

Test expectations:
- Super admin created with forcePasswordChange flag
- Admin route guard redirects when flag is true
- After password change, flag is cleared and admin is accessible
- Keyboard navigable and screen reader compatible form

tasks.title: Audit billing routes to identify all routes lacking ownership checks
tasks.description:
Perform a thorough audit of all billing routes in the API to identify which routes are missing ownership verification (IDOR vulnerability).

Files to audit:
- `apps/api/src/routes/billing/` -- all route files
- `apps/api/src/routes/index.ts` -- billing route mounting
- Check the difference between `/protected/billing/` and `/admin/billing/` routes

Steps:
1. List all billing route handlers and their HTTP methods
2. For each route, determine: Does it access a resource by ID? Does it verify the requesting user owns that resource?
3. Document findings in a list:
   - Route path + method
   - Has ownership check: yes/no
   - Should be admin-only: yes/no
   - Current tier: public/protected/admin
4. Identify routes that should be admin-only but are currently in the protected tier
5. Identify routes that accept a resource ID but don't verify ownership

Output: A documented list of all billing routes and their IDOR status, to be used by T-015 through T-019.

Test expectations:
- Complete inventory of all billing routes with ownership check status

tasks.title: Create billing ownership verification middleware
tasks.description:
Create a reusable middleware/guard function that verifies the requesting user owns the target billing resource, or has admin BILLING_MANAGE permission to bypass.

File to create/modify:
- `apps/api/src/middlewares/billing-ownership.ts` (new file) or add to existing billing middleware

Steps:
1. Create a middleware factory function `createBillingOwnershipGuard({ resourceType })` that:
   a. Extracts the resource ID from `c.req.param('id')` or the request body
   b. Queries the billing_customers table to find the customer record
   c. Compares the customer's userId with the authenticated user's ID (`c.get('actor').id`)
   d. If match: proceed (call `await next()`)
   e. If no match: check if actor has `PermissionEnum.BILLING_MANAGE` permission
   f. If has permission: proceed (admin bypass)
   g. Otherwise: return 403 Forbidden (or 404 to prevent enumeration)
2. Handle edge cases: resource not found (return 404), unauthenticated (return 401)
3. Support different resource types: customer, subscription, invoice, payment
4. For subscription/invoice/payment: look up the parent customer to determine ownership

Test expectations:
- Owner can access their own resource
- Non-owner gets 403/404
- Admin with BILLING_MANAGE can access any resource
- Unauthenticated gets 401
- Non-existent resource gets 404

tasks.title: Apply ownership middleware to billing customer routes
tasks.description:
Apply the billing ownership verification middleware (T-015) to all protected billing customer routes.

Files to modify:
- `apps/api/src/routes/billing/` -- customer route files under the protected tier

Steps:
1. Identify all customer routes: GET /protected/billing/customers/:id, PUT /protected/billing/customers/:id, etc.
2. Add the ownership middleware to each route that accesses a customer by ID
3. Ensure the middleware runs after authentication but before the route handler
4. For list endpoints (GET /protected/billing/customers), filter to only return the authenticated user's customer records

Test expectations:
- User A cannot GET User B's customer record (403 or 404)
- User A can GET their own customer record (200)
- Admin with BILLING_MANAGE can GET any customer (200)

tasks.title: Apply ownership middleware to billing subscription routes
tasks.description:
Apply the billing ownership verification middleware (T-015) to all protected billing subscription routes.

Files to modify:
- `apps/api/src/routes/billing/` -- subscription route files under the protected tier

Steps:
1. Identify all subscription routes: GET/PUT /protected/billing/subscriptions/:id, etc.
2. For subscription routes, the ownership check must look up the parent customer of the subscription to verify ownership
3. Add the ownership middleware with `resourceType: 'subscription'` to each route
4. Ensure PUT/PATCH operations also verify ownership

Test expectations:
- User A cannot PUT User B's subscription (403)
- User A can GET their own subscription (200)
- Admin bypass works correctly

tasks.title: Apply ownership middleware to billing invoice and payment routes
tasks.description:
Apply the billing ownership verification middleware (T-015) to all protected billing invoice and payment routes.

Files to modify:
- `apps/api/src/routes/billing/` -- invoice and payment route files under the protected tier

Steps:
1. Identify all invoice routes: GET /protected/billing/invoices/:id, POST /protected/billing/refunds, etc.
2. Identify all payment routes: GET /protected/billing/payments/:id, etc.
3. For invoice/payment routes, look up the parent customer via the subscription chain
4. Add ownership middleware to each route
5. Special attention to POST /protected/billing/refunds: verify the referenced invoice belongs to the requesting user

Test expectations:
- User A cannot POST a refund referencing User B's invoice (403)
- User A can view their own invoices (200)
- Admin bypass works for all operations

tasks.title: Migrate admin-only billing operations to /admin/billing/ with createAdminRoute
tasks.description:
Move billing operations that should only be accessible to admins from the protected tier to the admin tier, using the `createAdminRoute` pattern.

Files to modify:
- `apps/api/src/routes/billing/` -- identified admin-only routes from T-014 audit
- `apps/api/src/routes/index.ts` -- update route mounting

Steps:
1. From T-014 audit results, identify routes that should be admin-only (e.g., billing management, plan CRUD, etc.)
2. Move those route handlers to `/admin/billing/` path
3. Wrap with `createAdminRoute` and appropriate permission flags (e.g., `BILLING_MANAGE`)
4. Remove the old protected-tier mount points for these routes
5. Update any admin panel API calls if paths changed

Test expectations:
- Admin-only billing routes return 403 for non-admin users
- Admin with BILLING_MANAGE can access all admin billing routes
- Regular users can still access their own billing data via protected routes

tasks.title: Wrap metrics routes with createAdminRoute and permission flags
tasks.description:
Ensure all metrics endpoints require admin-level authorization using `createAdminRoute` with appropriate permission flags.

Files to modify:
- `apps/api/src/routes/metrics/index.ts` (or wherever metrics routes are defined)
- `apps/api/src/routes/index.ts` -- verify metrics are mounted under `/admin/metrics/`

Steps:
1. Locate all metrics route definitions
2. Verify they are mounted under `/api/v1/admin/metrics/`
3. Wrap each route with `createAdminRoute` if not already done
4. Set permission flags: `METRICS_VIEW` for GET endpoints, `METRICS_RESET` for POST reset endpoint
5. If `METRICS_VIEW` and `METRICS_RESET` permissions don't exist in `PermissionEnum`, they may need to be added or mapped to existing admin permissions
6. Verify the admin panel's metrics page still works with admin credentials

Test expectations:
- Regular authenticated user gets 403 on GET /admin/metrics
- Regular authenticated user gets 403 on POST /admin/metrics/reset
- Unauthenticated user gets 401
- Admin with METRICS_VIEW gets 200 on GET /admin/metrics
- Admin with METRICS_RESET gets 200 on POST /admin/metrics/reset

tasks.title: Write integration tests for billing IDOR and metrics authorization
tasks.description:
Write comprehensive integration tests covering all IDOR scenarios for billing routes and authorization for metrics routes.

File to create:
- `apps/api/test/integration/billing-idor.test.ts`
- `apps/api/test/integration/metrics-auth.test.ts`

Billing IDOR tests:
1. User A authenticated, GET /protected/billing/customers/:userBId -> 403 or 404
2. User A authenticated, GET /protected/billing/customers/:userAId -> 200
3. User A authenticated, PUT /protected/billing/subscriptions/:userBSubId -> 403
4. User A authenticated, POST /protected/billing/refunds with User B's invoice -> 403
5. Admin with BILLING_MANAGE, GET /admin/billing/customers/:anyId -> 200
6. Unauthenticated, GET /protected/billing/customers/:id -> 401
7. Owner reads own subscription, invoice, payment -> 200

Metrics auth tests:
1. Regular user, GET /admin/metrics -> 403
2. Regular user, POST /admin/metrics/reset -> 403
3. Unauthenticated, GET /admin/metrics -> 401
4. Admin with METRICS_VIEW, GET /admin/metrics -> 200
5. Admin with METRICS_RESET, POST /admin/metrics/reset -> 200

Test expectations:
- All tests use the AAA pattern
- Tests create necessary test fixtures (users, billing records)
- All assertions match the acceptance criteria from US-04 and US-05

tasks.title: Replace sql.raw() in billing-metrics.service.ts with parameterized query
tasks.description:
Eliminate the SQL injection risk by replacing `sql.raw(months.toString())` with a parameterized Drizzle interval expression.

File to modify:
- `apps/api/src/services/billing-metrics.service.ts`

Steps:
1. Locate the `sql.raw(months.toString())` usage in the INTERVAL expression
2. Replace with a Drizzle-native parameterized approach. Options:
   a. Use `sql`\`INTERVAL '1 month' * ${months}\`` (Drizzle template literal with parameter binding)
   b. Or compute the date boundary in JavaScript and pass it as a parameterized date value
3. Ensure the `months` value is bound as a parameter, never interpolated into the SQL string

Test expectations:
- Passing `months = 6` produces the correct interval query
- No `sql.raw()` calls remain in the file
- Query results are identical to before the change

tasks.title: Add input validation for months parameter in billing-metrics
tasks.description:
Add strict input validation for the `months` parameter in billing-metrics to prevent any injection or abuse.

File to modify:
- `apps/api/src/services/billing-metrics.service.ts`

Steps:
1. Add Zod validation for the `months` parameter: `z.number().int().min(1).max(120)` (or appropriate range)
2. Validate at the service entry point before any database query
3. Return a typed validation error if the value is invalid
4. Write unit tests:
   a. `months = 6` -> valid, proceeds
   b. `months = 0` -> validation error
   c. `months = -1` -> validation error
   d. `months = 1.5` -> validation error (not integer)
   e. `months = '1; DROP TABLE billing_subscriptions'` -> validation error (not number)
   f. `months = 200` -> validation error (exceeds max)

Test expectations:
- Invalid values never reach the database
- Valid values produce correct results
- Unit tests cover all edge cases listed above

tasks.title: Create Redis rate limit store implementation
tasks.description:
Create a Redis-backed rate limit store to replace the in-memory Map, ensuring rate limits work across multiple API instances.

File to create:
- `apps/api/src/lib/rate-limit-store.ts` (or similar)

Steps:
1. Check if the project already has a Redis client (search for `ioredis`, `redis`, or `HOSPEDA_REDIS_URL` usage)
2. If no Redis client exists, determine which library to use (ioredis is commonly used)
3. Create a rate limit store class/module that:
   a. Connects to Redis using `HOSPEDA_REDIS_URL`
   b. Implements `increment(key: string, windowMs: number): Promise<{ count: number; resetTime: number }>`
   c. Uses Redis `INCR` + `EXPIRE` (or `MULTI/EXEC`) for atomic increment with TTL
   d. TTL-based expiration handles cleanup automatically (no manual cleanup code)
   e. Key format: `ratelimit:<identifier>:<window>`
4. Handle Redis connection failures gracefully (fallback to allow or use a circuit breaker)
5. Export the store for use by the rate limit middleware

Test expectations:
- Redis store correctly increments counters
- Counters expire after the TTL window
- Multiple increments within a window accumulate correctly
- Connection failure is handled without crashing the server

tasks.title: Replace in-memory Map rate limiter with Redis implementation
tasks.description:
Replace the in-memory `Map` rate limiter in the rate limit middleware with the Redis-backed implementation from T-024.

File to modify:
- `apps/api/src/middlewares/rate-limit.ts`

Steps:
1. Import the Redis rate limit store from T-024
2. Remove the in-memory `Map` variable and all related cleanup logic
3. Replace the rate limit check to use the Redis store's `increment` method
4. Use the returned count and resetTime to determine if the request should be throttled
5. Set `Retry-After` and `X-RateLimit-*` headers based on the Redis store's data
6. Ensure `HOSPEDA_REDIS_URL` is documented in `.env.example` as required for production

Test expectations:
- No in-memory `Map` exists in the rate limit middleware
- Rate limit headers are correctly set
- Requests exceeding the threshold receive 429 Too Many Requests
- Rate limits are enforced consistently (verified by Redis key inspection in tests)

tasks.title: Add non-root user to Dockerfile.api
tasks.description:
Add a non-root user to the API Dockerfile so the container process does not run as root.

File to modify:
- `apps/api/Dockerfile.api` (or `Dockerfile` in apps/api/)

Steps:
1. Locate the Dockerfile for the API app
2. Before the `CMD` instruction, add:
   ```dockerfile
   RUN addgroup --system --gid 1001 hospeda && \
       adduser --system --uid 1001 --ingroup hospeda hospeda
   USER hospeda
   ```
3. Ensure the application's working directory and any log/temp directories are writable by the non-root user:
   ```dockerfile
   RUN chown -R hospeda:hospeda /app
   ```
   (Adjust paths as needed based on the existing Dockerfile structure)
4. Build the image and verify: `docker build -t hospeda-api-test . && docker run --rm hospeda-api-test whoami` should output `hospeda`
5. Verify the application starts without permission errors

Test expectations:
- `docker inspect` shows default user is not root (not UID 0)
- `whoami` inside container returns `hospeda` (or `node`)
- Application starts and can write to logs/temp without errors

N/A: Migrated from Docker/Fly.io to Vercel serverless deployment.

**OBSOLETE**: Dockerfile.api is deprecated. API migrated to Vercel serverless where container user context is not applicable. Vercel handles security isolation at the platform level.

tasks.title: Gate docs routes behind NODE_ENV check in production
tasks.description:
Prevent Swagger/Scalar API documentation endpoints from being accessible in production.

Files to modify:
- `apps/api/src/index.ts` or `apps/api/src/app.ts` -- wherever docs routes are registered
- Look for `/docs`, `/reference`, `/ui` route registrations

Steps:
1. Locate where Scalar/Swagger/OpenAPI documentation routes are registered
2. Wrap the registration in a `NODE_ENV` check:
   ```typescript
   if (process.env.NODE_ENV !== 'production') {
     // Register docs routes
   }
   ```
3. Optionally, for production environments where docs access is needed internally, add an API key guard:
   ```typescript
   if (process.env.HOSPEDA_DOCS_API_KEY) {
     // Register docs behind API key auth
   }
   ```
4. Verify in development: `/docs` returns the documentation UI
5. Verify with `NODE_ENV=production`: `/docs` returns 404

Test expectations:
- In development: docs endpoints return 200 with documentation UI
- In production (NODE_ENV=production): docs endpoints return 404
- If HOSPEDA_DOCS_API_KEY is set in production: docs accessible with correct Authorization header

tasks.title: Fix wildcard subdomain matching in security.ts
tasks.description:
Fix the CORS origin validation to properly reject sibling domains that end with the allowed domain suffix (e.g., `evil-hospeda.com.ar` should not match `*.hospeda.com.ar`).

File to modify:
- `apps/api/src/middlewares/security.ts`

Steps:
1. Locate the wildcard origin matching logic in the CORS configuration
2. Find the pattern that checks if an origin matches a wildcard like `*.hospeda.com.ar`
3. The current implementation likely uses `origin.endsWith('hospeda.com.ar')` which incorrectly matches `evil-hospeda.com.ar`
4. Fix to check for `.` prefix: `origin.endsWith('.hospeda.com.ar')` or use a proper subdomain regex: `/^https?:\/\/[a-z0-9-]+\.hospeda\.com\.ar$/`
5. Handle apex domain (`hospeda.com.ar`) separately in an explicit allowlist
6. Write unit tests for the matching function

Test expectations:
- `evil-hospeda.com.ar` -> rejected (no CORS headers)
- `admin.hospeda.com.ar` -> allowed
- `sub.admin.hospeda.com.ar` -> allowed (nested subdomain)
- `hospeda.com.ar` -> allowed if in explicit allowlist
- `notahospeda.com.ar` -> rejected

tasks.title: Filter error details on 5xx responses in production
tasks.description:
Ensure internal error details (stack traces, query details, internal messages) are not exposed in 5xx error responses in production.

File to modify:
- `apps/api/src/middlewares/response.ts` (or the error handler middleware)

Steps:
1. Locate the error handler/response middleware that formats error responses
2. Add a production check: `const isProduction = process.env.NODE_ENV === 'production'`
3. For 5xx errors (status >= 500):
   a. In production: omit the `details` field or replace with `'An unexpected error occurred'`
   b. In development: keep full details for debugging
4. For 4xx errors: keep `details` field (field-level validation messages are safe to expose)
5. Ensure the full error is still logged server-side (via logger) even when filtered from the response

Test expectations:
- 5xx in production: response has no `details` or generic message only
- 5xx in development: response includes full diagnostic `details`
- 4xx in production: `details` field with validation info is preserved
- Server-side logging still captures the full error

tasks.title: Update cron middleware to block when CRON_SECRET not set
tasks.description:
Change the cron middleware to always require `CRON_SECRET` authentication, blocking requests when the secret is not configured (instead of allowing them).

File to modify:
- `apps/api/src/cron/middleware.ts` (or wherever cron auth is handled)

Steps:
1. Locate the cron authentication middleware
2. Remove any `CRON_AUTH_DISABLED` bypass logic, or restrict it to `NODE_ENV !== 'production'` with a logged warning
3. When `CRON_SECRET` is not set:
   a. In production: refuse to register cron routes (or return 401 on all cron requests)
   b. In non-production: log a warning and return 401 (block by default, not allow)
4. When `CRON_SECRET` is set: validate `Authorization: Bearer <CRON_SECRET>` header
5. Invalid or missing header: return 401 Unauthorized

Test expectations:
- No CRON_SECRET set + any request -> 401
- CRON_SECRET set + correct Bearer header -> request processed
- CRON_SECRET set + wrong Bearer header -> 401
- CRON_SECRET set + no Authorization header -> 401

tasks.title: Add CRON_SECRET to env schema as required for production
tasks.description:

Steps:

2. Add `CRON_SECRET` as a string field
3. Add a refinement or conditional validation: required when `NODE_ENV === 'production'`, optional otherwise
4. Update `.env.example` to document `CRON_SECRET` as required for production

Test expectations:
- In production without CRON_SECRET: env validation fails with descriptive error
- In production with CRON_SECRET: validation passes
- In development without CRON_SECRET: validation passes (optional)
- In development with CRON_SECRET: validation passes

tasks.title: Create shared sanitizeHtml utility for Astro pages
tasks.description:
Create a shared HTML sanitization utility to prevent XSS from API-sourced content rendered with `set:html`. File to create: - `apps/web/src/utils/sanitize-html.ts` Steps: 1. Choose a server-safe sanitization library: `sanitize-html` or `isomorphic-dompurify` 2. Add the dependency to `apps/web/package.json` 3. Create the utility function: ```typescript import sanitizeHtml from 'sanitize-html'; export function sanitize({ html }: { html: string }): string { return sanitizeHtml(html, { allowedTags: ['strong', 'em', 'p', 'a', 'ul', 'ol', 'li', 'br', 'h1', 'h2', 'h3', 'h4', 'h5', 'h6', 'blockquote'], allowedAttributes: { 'a': ['href', 'target', 'rel'] }, disallowedTagsMode: 'discard' }); } ``` 4. Write unit tests: a. `` -> stripped b. `` -> attribute stripped c. `bold` -> preserved d. `

paragraph

` -> preserved e. `link` -> preserved Test expectations: - Script tags and inline event handlers are always stripped - Legitimate formatting HTML is preserved - Function follows RO-RO pattern

tasks.title: Apply sanitizeHtml to all Astro pages using set:html
tasks.description:
Find all Astro pages/components that use `set:html` with dynamic API-sourced content and wrap the content through the sanitization utility.

Files to modify:
- All `.astro` files in `apps/web/src/` that use `set:html` with dynamic content

Steps:
1. Search for all `set:html` usages: `grep -r 'set:html' apps/web/src/`
2. For each usage, determine if the content comes from an API response or user-generated data
3. If API-sourced, wrap with the sanitize function:
   ```astro
   ---
   import { sanitize } from '@/utils/sanitize-html';
   const cleanHtml = sanitize({ html: apiContent });
   ---
   <div set:html={cleanHtml} />
   ```
4. If the content is hardcoded/static, no change needed
5. Create a checklist of all `set:html` usages and their sanitization status

Test expectations:
- Every `set:html` with dynamic content passes through sanitize()
- No raw API content is directly set as HTML without sanitization
- Static/hardcoded HTML is not unnecessarily sanitized

tasks.title: Replace Content-Length body size check with Hono bodyLimit middleware
Refactor permission.ts to use PermissionEnum instead of role checks
tasks.description:
Replace all `actor.role === RoleEnum.ADMIN` (or similar role comparisons) in the permission utility with `PermissionEnum`-based checks.

File to modify:
- `packages/service-core/src/utils/permission.ts`

Steps:
1. Read the file and identify all instances of role-based checks (e.g., `actor.role === RoleEnum.ADMIN`, `actor.role === RoleEnum.SUPER_ADMIN`, `actor.role !== RoleEnum.USER`)
2. For each role check, determine the equivalent permission check:
   - `actor.role === RoleEnum.ADMIN` -> `actor.permissions.includes(PermissionEnum.ADMIN_ACCESS)` (or similar)
   - Map each role-based decision to the specific permission that should control it
3. Refactor each check to use `hasPermission(actor, PermissionEnum.XXX)` or equivalent utility
4. Ensure no `RoleEnum` comparisons remain in the file
5. Run existing unit tests to verify behavior is preserved

Test expectations:
- No `actor.role ===` comparisons in the file
- All authorization decisions use PermissionEnum
- Existing permission tests pass
- An actor with admin role but missing a specific permission is correctly denied

tasks.title: Refactor accommodation.service.ts role checks to permission checks

   - Read access: `PermissionEnum.ACCOMMODATION_READ` or similar
   - Write access: `PermissionEnum.ACCOMMODATION_WRITE` or `ACCOMMODATION_MANAGE`
   - Delete access: `PermissionEnum.ACCOMMODATION_DELETE` or `ACCOMMODATION_MANAGE`
3. Replace each `actor.role === RoleEnum.XXX` with the permission-based check

tasks.title: Filter /auth/me response to exclude full permission array

- `apps/api/src/routes/auth/me.ts` (or wherever the auth/me endpoint is defined)

Steps:

2. Create a response schema or field whitelist:
   ```typescript
   const MeResponseSchema = z.object({
     id: z.string(),
     email: z.string(),
     name: z.string(),
     role: z.string(),
     image: z.string().nullable().optional(),
     // Only include permissions the client needs for UI rendering
   });
   ```
3. Before returning the response, filter the actor object through the schema:
   ```typescript
   const filtered = MeResponseSchema.parse(actor);
   return c.json(filtered);
   ```
4. Ensure the admin panel still receives enough data to render (role, display name, avatar)
5. Verify the web app still functions correctly with the filtered response

Test expectations:

tasks.title: Document cookie cache tradeoff in auth.ts comments
tasks.description:
Add documentation comments to the auth configuration explaining the 5-minute cookie cache propagation tradeoff (SEC-17 accepted risk).

File to modify:
- `apps/api/src/lib/auth.ts`

Steps:
1. Locate the cookie cache configuration in `auth.ts`
2. Add a JSDoc comment block explaining:
   a. The 5-minute session cache means permission changes take up to 5 minutes to propagate
   b. This is an accepted tradeoff between performance and immediate revocation
   c. If an admin revokes a user's role, the user retains access for up to 5 minutes
   d. Recommendation: consider reducing to 1-2 minutes in a future iteration
   e. Reference: SPEC-019, SEC-17
3. Keep the comment concise but informative

Example:
```typescript
/**
 * Cookie cache TTL: 5 minutes.
 * SECURITY NOTE (SEC-17): Permission changes (e.g., role revocation) take up to
 * 5 minutes to propagate due to this cache. This is an accepted tradeoff for
 * performance. Consider reducing to 1-2 minutes if near-realtime revocation
 * becomes a requirement. See SPEC-019.
 */
```

Test expectations:
- Comment exists near the cookie cache configuration
- No functional code changes

3. Document any other findings from T-046 (password field audit) if issues were found and accepted
4. For each accepted risk, include:
   a. Finding ID and description
   b. Why it is accepted (business/technical rationale)

Test expectations:

- No functional code changes

tasks.title: Manual verification of all user stories on staging
tasks.description:
Deploy to staging and manually verify all user story acceptance criteria from SPEC-019.

Checklist:
- [ ] `npm audit --production` shows zero critical/high advisories
- [ ] `git log --all -- .env` shows no credential values
- [ ] Super admin seed generates random password when env var absent, prints to stdout
- [ ] Super admin prompted to change password on first login
- [ ] Non-owner user gets 403 accessing another user's billing data
- [ ] Non-admin user gets 403 on all metrics endpoints
- [ ] API docs (/docs, /reference, /ui) return 404 in production
- [ ] HTTP request redirected to HTTPS on Fly.io
- [ ] Docker container runs as non-root user (verified with `docker exec whoami`)
- [ ] Non-admin authenticated user redirected when accessing admin app routes
- [ ] /auth/me response does not include full permission array
- [ ] Chunked request exceeding body limit returns 413
- [ ] Cron endpoint without CRON_SECRET returns 401
- [ ] Exchange rate routes on correct tiers (public/protected/admin)
- [ ] CORS rejects `evil-hospeda.com.ar` but allows `admin.hospeda.com.ar`

Test expectations:
- All checklist items verified and passing

**DEFERRED to SPEC-025** (Staging Environment Setup & Deployment Pipeline). Manual verification requires a staging environment which is tracked in SPEC-025.
